item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This mental image is fantastic.

You tell a D-class to trust you, then tell him very seriously to jump through the paper tower. Don't worry, there's a net at the bottom, you'll be fine. Reluctant, but not so naive as to think the Foundation actually wants him dead, he leaps, and immediately becomes toothpaste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might be good material for a test log supplement if you ever want to consider it but I see what you mean. It's cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I'm going to edit the article to include this since it's at a good length and well-received as-is, but I just want you to know how much I absolutely love this and the fact that you wrote it.

I think the whole 'violently shifting' thing is how it would go about this — if the laws of physics need to bend to accomodate the test, then so be it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I have a very… Probably odd but very good way to test how much physics can be broken to make the score over time function break everything.

The test is taken by gravity. There is a white board in front of a 1m^2 sheet of paper, held up and divided into 4 evenly with steel bars for the 4 choices, written on the center of the square which is drawn. Answering correctly means that you fell through the correct square. The D class who is literate is instructed to try to read the questions and answers of the test as it falls by jumping into the center of 1 specific square, like say C. Assuming 2 meters (m) between each layer for a 120m tower, and if the test has answers randomly arranged, normally 25% would be the score, but it would take 4.95s seconds total to "complete" this test. So their score should be 0 or something extremely odd like "subject's mass violently shifted to avoid the correct answers during falling. The subject should have and did indeed go through the final answer paper at around 4.95 seconds, but only 4.125% of the mass of what remains there were, caused by the violent forces on the body, went through the correct square. This would give his body as a whole a score of 1/60 but since only 4.125% went through the final answer correctly his score is .04125/60 if the score was based on the mass falling through."

Physics: the equation for falling would be on a mass with earth's gravity normally, assuming no air resistance: sqrt ( 2 * height / 9.8 ), with 120m being 4.948717 seconds. Hmm but I'm not sure what and where the forces pounded on the body to prevent all mass from avoiding the correct answer. Might require some [DATA EXPUNGED] for those details.

Here's a drawing kind of explaining how the test looks with 1 question at the top and as you fall (pretend the question is a big box on top and the answers are 4 squares that make up a bigger square):

QUESTION
ANSWER A ANSWER B
ANSWER C ANSWER D

Feel free to use the idea or not, however you want to. Might be a real bear to word correctly even with some details expunged and the little table drawing I just made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No problem! I think the new wording clearly conveys the effect and its anomalous nature.

"And I halped!"
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bB7j3sUWohE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No worries. That's a reasonable point of confusion, and while I didn't adopt your exact wording, I did make a few edits to clear the matter up. It was sort of an artifact from an earlier version anyways.

Thanks for the help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the vagueness, I was trying to post quickly. Anyway, I have achieved enlightenment. What I couldn't understand was why scoring a test based on the length of time spent on it was anomalous. It wasn't clear to me that all the weird tests were trying to force a different number of correct answers than the exam duration should permit. Suggested edit: Change

Regardless of aptitude or other obstructions, any human who attempts to complete SCP-2496 will receive a score equal to exactly half of the time (measured in minutes and rounded to the nearest minute) taken to complete the test, with two hours being sufficient for a perfect score. For example, a person who spends forty minutes on SCP-2496 will receive twenty points out of sixty, without exception. Spending more than 120 minutes on the test results in a score of 60. Highly improbable events, unusual behavior, and direct violation of the laws of physics have been noted during testing. See attached testing logs for additional information.

to

Regardless of aptitude or other obstructions, any human who attempts to complete SCP-2496 will answer one question correctly for every two minutes (rounding up) spent on the test, with two hours being sufficient for a perfect score of 60 out of 60. Highly improbable events, unusual behavior, and direct violation of the laws of physics have been noted during attempts to break the connection between time spent on SCP-2496 and the number of correct answers. See attached testing logs for additional information.

Whaddya think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My professor told us to take our time on a final exam so we would do better, and I thought it would be interesting if test performance was tied more directly to the time spent. The intent was to take that idea and follow it to its logical conclusion.

I admit I'm kind of at a loss with regards to interpreting this. Did the piece not feel like a coherent whole? Was it aimless? Too simple? I've never considered 'intent' to be part of an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no freaking idea what the intent of this SCP is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this article. It is so amusing to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the meaning of the title? I looked up 'parallel postulate', and am I to understand that the author meant it as a metaphor for "the subject will eventually get the expected score"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. As of this post, there are 6 tests but only 4 subjects (D-7278 and D-6918) are used twice. Why wouldn't they bring in different subjects for every test?
  2. Can someone please explain D-6918's 2nd attempt? I get the gist. It's a clever attempt to force the SCP to break reality. But the subject got FULL points which defies the anomalous behavior.
  3. What if the subject had all the time they needed to take the test but was unable to answer the questions (like, their pen had no ink). How would the SCP break physics to get the subject to answer enough questions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's funny that you snuck a subtly abstandard format into a well written article and that a very small fraction of the people reading it are manically raging while most other folks upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They will receive a score equal to exactly half of the time (measured in minutes and rounded to the nearest minute) taken to complete the test. As far as we/I know, any path that leads from the premise to this conclusion could be valid. There is a logic behind exactly what happens in each case, but it is far beyond our capabilities to suss it out.

In other words, use your imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if the test is given to someone who doesn't understand English, or the concept of how to fill out a standardized test? Someone like, for instance, SCP-163?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Context for the uninformed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loads of fun to be had with this one. Dont let helixsnake hear about this one.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Laughed at "subject expired. Five points were awarded". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how testing gets progressivly more insane, yet somehow makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well…

It's an excellently1 well written piece of literature, sure, and I've done enough standardized tests as to see where you're getting. But I don't feel anything in particular about it. I understand it, I believe, and it is engaging enough as to make me keep to it, but I'm not sure whether to upvote or no-vote.

Yet. And I've read it twice now.

Just let me mull it over. I'll come around. :3

Footnotes
1. For an average Foundation writer. For a Scantron-quality Foundation writer, it's… a Scantron piece.

I can't very well compare Scantron pieces with anyone around here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what to feel about this.

Subject given a cheat sheet for SCP-2496 and told that the feelings described there were needed to complete SCP-2496 correctly. Subject appropriately fulfills SCP-2496 within two hours.

Oh, I know what to feel about this!

I need to re-read this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not necessarily a Scantron test.

But it can be one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scantron writes about a scantron

Brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"So what genre of music do you play?"
"Anomalous metal."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude. This just made me think of a band name: "Anomaly Class: Apollyon." Just, apocalyptic Black Metal and Symphonic Metal. It will be the most Metal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Credits roll; instead of actors, each is a multiple-choice question. The credits don't move on until you answer them. You can't move until you answer them. Your vision is turning red.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) They get thirty points. It's a matter of how much time they do take, not how much time they could take. The tests given in the article just happen to involve people going right up to the time limit most of the time. This is demonstrated in the first test, where the D-class is given as much time as they please, but only use 108 minutes, and thus only get 54/60.

2) That's a good point and something I hadn't considered. I'll edit in a line saying that taking more than two hours doesn't increase the score past 60/60.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two questions that came to mind:

1) Does a person always have to take the max time given to complete the test? For example if a person is given two hours but finishes it in 1 hour, what happens?

2) What happens if a person is given more than 2 hours to complete the test?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great! A simple skip with all the meat in the test logs, precisely what you'd expect from series I! Oh, except for the "Anomaly Class". That's our Scantron! laughtrack

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomaly Class

this is completely UNACCEPTABLE stuff like this is the literal CANCER that is destroying the scp foundation I hope you are ASHAMED

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3edgy5me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are free to do whatever you wish in your articles, and we are free to vote accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'all realize that you're just encouraging me, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, a self-serving format screw is enough of a reason for a downvote from me. Vote reversed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomaly Class: Safe

Rabble rabble rabble

I know it seems kind of petty, but I'm not a fan of changing the format just for the sake of changing it. I only approve of format-altering if you can justify it artistically, and there's no reason this can't be "Object Class." (Yes, I know your reasoning for preferring "Anomaly." No, I don't agree with it.)

However, the article is well written otherwise. So, neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think at a certain point the 'path of least resistance' would just be answering the test in such a way that there's somebody with much easier standards who is able and willing to grade the test, or manipulating probability and physics and whatnot such that that happens. Sort of like a very loose conservation of "energy", in a way. It all depends on the logic that 2496 runs on, which isn't always obvious.

Of course, even if the Foundation was inclined to push their luck in this respect, I suspect that the results of the test wouldn't be made available to the clearance being used here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These seems super exploitable.

"Finish the test in two hours marking your answer with a solution one of these open problems in mathematics per question."
"… by assassinating one of these four enemies of the Foundation to show your solution."

Still, nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing I can think of during the bow and arrow test is "Patriot Arrow?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Standardized tests are cancer…

Actually, no, cancer might be curable and not everyone experiences it firsthand. You could easily make a joke SCP on the premise of standardized tests being objectively awful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scantron, you mad genius. +1
It also helps that I just took an insane amount of standardized tests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been sitting in my pastebin since the tail end of 2013, despite being mostly finished. I changed the one test I wasn't happy with, polished it up, and posted it.

Enjoy.
